EXPERIMENT The experimental study intends to answer the following questions: •RQ1: (1) How effective is ourCARROTfor the cost- constrained RAG pipeline compared to other methods? (2) DoesCARROTconsistently maintain its effectiveness across different LLMs? •RQ2: How efficient isCARROTcompared to different baselines? •RQ3: How doesCARROTperform with large-scale datasets when evaluating its scalability? •RQ4: How does the performance ofCARROTvary across different cost budget constraints? •RQ5: What is the effectiveness of each component in CARROT? A. Experimental Setting Environment.We integrate our framework with the RAG framework LlamaIndex [32] and Faiss [55]. Experiments used dual Intel Xeon Gold 6326 CPUs (32 cores, 64 threads), 512 GB DDR4 RAM, and 960 GB NVMe SSD. The con- figuration agent is implemented in PyTorch 2.4, with data collection and training performed on 8×A5000 GPUs. We choose OpenRouter [56] to deploy our inference service. Datasets.To evaluate the performance ofCARROTacross di- verse scenarios, we conduct experiments on three datasets with differing task focuses (Table I): (1)WikiPassageQA[57] is a question-answering benchmark containing 4,165 questions and over 100,000 text chunks, designed for non-factoid question answering and passage retrieval. (2)MARCO[58] is a compre- hensive dataset tailored for natural language processing tasks, primarily emphasizing question answering and retrieval. (3) HotpotQA[59] is a question-answering benchmark designed for multi-hop reasoning tasks, consisting of approximately 113K QA pairs sourced from Wikipedia. Baselines.We compareCARROTagainst representative rank- based RAG methods, includingRAPTOR[6],NaiveRAG[28], HYDE[29], andFLARE[17], to demonstrate improvements in retrieval relevance and response quality. We also evaluate graph-based approaches such asGraphRAG[13],Graph- CoT[25], andG-Retriever[60], highlighting our efficiency advantages. Finally, we include comparisons with tuning-based methods, despite their substantial computational overhead and infrastructure requirements. For instance,ColBERT[23] re- quires 3 hours to index 9M passages on four V100 GPUs, whileRankRAG[14] andSelf-RAG[21] demand 128 A100 GPUs for 16 hours and 4 A100 GPUs for 12 hours, re- spectively. We therefore includeColBERTas a representative baseline for fairness, noting that such tuning-based methods are often impractical for large-scale deployment due to their prohibitive training and indexing costs. Details of all baselines are provided in our technical report [40]. In addition, we remove the configuration agent from our method as a baseline to evaluate its impact on the performance ofCARROT, referring to this version asCARROT w/o Agent. Finally, we implement a method calledCARROT Upperto establish an upper bound by enumerating all possible chunk combinations within the top-5 chunk candidates and selecting the optimal chunk combination order. We adopt Llama3- 8B [61] as the default large language model for these methods. Hyper-parameter Settings.The hyper-parameters forCAR- ROTare automatically determined by the configuration agent, whileNaiveRAGdoes not require hyper-parameters. For other baseline methods, we ensure consistency by using identical hyper-parameters for fair comparisons. Specifically, we set the exploration coefficient to 2.4, the number of iterations to 10, and the cost coefficientλto 0.1.

Due to the computational constraints imposed by combinatorial complexity, we set the candidate chunks to 5 in theCARROT Uppercase. By default, we set the fixed chunk size to 256 and the budget to 1024 for fair ablation studies. For offline data collection, we sample 1K seed queries from three datasets and perform grid search over 6 rerankers with 20 parameter combinations per query. Learning Setting.In our method, the configuration agent is trained using contrastive learning. The hyper-parameters used during this process include a margin for contrastive loss (margin=1.0), learning rate (lr=0.001), batch size (32), number of epochs (num epochs=60), and the embedding model (i.e., BAAI/bge-m3 [54]). Evaluation Metrics.Following [12], [14], [15], we utilize ROUGE-1, ROUGE-L [62], and BLEU-1 [63] for single- hop retrieval tasks, and F1 score [64] for multi-hop retrieval scenarios. To evaluate efficiency, we measure the retrieval latency across different methods. B. D. Case Study Figure 12 presents qualitative examples comparing the re- trieval performance ofCARROTagainst traditional baselines, illustrating how our method effectively addresses key chal- lenges:(1) Importance of Chunk Combination Order.To evaluate the impact of chunk combination order, we test a deliberately reordered version ofCARROT’s output. The result shows a significant performance drop, with the ROUGE-1 score declining from 0.43 to 0.21, confirming that the chunk combination order is critical for RAG inference. (2) Non-monotonic Utility of Chunk Selection.We compare CARROTwith baselines such asRAPTOR,NaiveRAG, and HYDE, all of which consume the full 8192 online input tokens but underperform relative toCARROT, which uses only 899 online input tokens. For instance,NaiveRAGrelies on top-kretrieval with reranking but greedily selects chunks without considering their correlations, often failing to capture semantically relevant context. When answering the query”Is bougainvillea a shrub?”, it retrieves text containing match- ing terms but misses the essential taxonomic information. Meanwhile,HYDEandRAPTORdepend on costly LLM calls to generate hypotheses or summarize content, leading to inefficient token usage.

In contrast,CARROT’s adaptive chunk selection strategy retrieves concise, semantically aligned context, enabling more accurate and efficient LLM responses. (3) Handling Query Diversity.To assess robustness to di- verse query types, we compareCARROTwith its variant CARROT w/o Agent, which lacks the adaptive configuration agent. Without this component, the model struggles to identify optimal chunk combinations and often fails to retrieve the most relevant information, demonstrating the importance of the configuration agent in handling varied query intents. VIII. CONCLUSION Considering non-monotonic chunk utility, chunk correla- tion, and diverse query domains, we proposeCARROT, a learned cost-constrained retrieval optimization framework for RAG. We model chunk combination orders as a policy tree and apply MCTS to find the optimal combination within a cost constraint. A configuration agent predicts the best query configuration and reranker, while a parallel expansion strategy processes multiple nodes per iteration. Comprehensive experiments across three benchmark datasets demonstrate that CARROTsignificantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods under constrained costs, achieving up to 30% improvement while maintaining superior efficiency and scalability.
